title: 5 takeaways from STAT's series on the soaring cost of health insurance
summary: STAT reporter Bob Herman published a summary of the 'Out of Pocket, Out of Reach' series examining employer-based health insurance in America. More than 150 million Americans obtain coverage through employment, making it the dominant insurance channel. Fewer than 60% of firms with 200 or fewer workers now offer health benefits—a historic low driven by soaring premium costs and rising deductibles that are over 50% higher at small firms than at large employers. Small businesses also face unexpected surcharges from insurers when enrollment grows too quickly, further discouraging coverage offerings. The full series delves into premiums outpacing wage growth, shrinking specialist networks limiting patient access to specialty medicines, and the role of CMS in driving up Medicare Advantage costs.
analysis_notes: Article content was truncated by paywall—only two of the five expected takeaways were rendered. Most of the series' detailed analysis remained inaccessible. Tags and entities below were extracted from summary text, topic links, and sidebar recommendations. Prioritized as low because no specific drug approvals, deals, or trial results are discussed; the piece surveys macro insurance cost trends relevant to biotech operators mainly through patient affordability lens.

More than 150 million Americans get their health insurance from a job, making it the primary way people in the U.S. get coverage. STAT is [examining this crucial source of insurance](https://www.statnews.com/health-insurance-costs-out-of-pocket-out-of-reach-series) to understand how and why it is becoming unaffordable for workers and their families across the country.

## Health benefits are fading away at small companies

Nearly all large companies offer health insurance. But fewer than 60% of smaller firms, with 200 or fewer workers, now offer insurance, an all-time low. The [costs of health insurance](https://www.statnews.com/2026/07/07/small-business-health-insurance-costs-out-of-pocket-series-part-1/) are pushing small businesses — beloved by politicians and the bedrocks of entire communities — to a financial cliff. Watering down their insurance plans, or deciding not to offer insurance at all, makes it more difficult for these businesses to retain employees.

Advertisement

## Offering insurance comes with huge out-of-pocket costs and unwelcome surprises

Even though most small businesses still offer health insurance, they are burdened most by the costs. Deductibles are more than 50% higher at small firms compared with larger companies. Small businesses also could face unexpected surcharges from insurance companies if the number of workers who enroll increases too much.

title: Ebola outbreak exposes a fractured outbreak response system
summary: STAT News reporters Allison DeAngelis and Helen Branswell report that the ongoing Ebola outbreak in Africa is exposing critical weaknesses in the global public health response infrastructure. The article uses the situation at Congo's General Hospital in Bunia, where a treatment center is under construction, as a focal point to examine systemic gaps in outbreak preparedness, funding, and coordination. The morning-rounds format also covers secondary health topics including rising employer-based health care costs threatening small businesses' ability to offer coverage, evolving profiles of the Gen Z medical workforce, and broader questions about the affordability of American health care. The piece appears to sit within a wider STAT series on the Ebola crisis, alongside related coverage on whether outbreaks can be contained and the emerging race for therapeutic development.
analysis_notes: Full article content was behind STAT+ paywall. Extracted metadata: headline, subtitle, author bios and topics (infectious disease, DRC/ Uganda outbreak, health care costs, Gen Z doctors). Sidebar linked articles provide context on Ebola therapy development and broader outbreak containment concerns. Summary relies on article title, subtitle, image caption identifying Bunia Congo as the location, and related article headlines visible in sidebars.

title: Pharmalittle: We're reading about a $10 billion Vertex deal, the pace of FDA drug approvals, and more
summary: Vertex Pharmaceuticals will pay $10 billion to acquire Crinetics Pharmaceuticals and its drug Palsonify, approved for treating acromegaly, a rare endogeneity disorder. The deal marks the largest acquisition in Vertex's history as the Boston-based company seeks to diversify beyond its cystic fibrosis portfolio, with additional candidates including one for congenital adrenal hyperplasia under development. The digest also covers a JAMA-released secret shopper study by Yale and other researchers finding that telehealth prescribing of GLP-1 obesity drugs often lacks adequate clinical oversight, enabling fast, easy prescriptions with minimal screening. Meanwhile, colleague Bob Herman launched a new investigative series examining how rising health insurance costs are increasingly crushing workers and small businesses. The newsletter also announced Andrew Joseph will anchor STAT's coverage from the Alzheimer's Association International Conference in London.
analysis_notes: Full article body is behind STAT+ paywall. Summary constructed from headline, lead paragraph (Vertex-Crinetics deals), teaser text snippets visible before the error frame, sidebar links, and newsletter intro paragraph mentioning GLP-1 telehealth study by JAMA/Yale researchers and Bob Herman insurance cost series.

Good day here from STAT’s London outpost, with Andrew Joseph filling in for Mr. Pharmalot. Maybe the less said about the U.S. World Cup match the better, though watching it with the time difference has put us in need of an extra cup or two of stimulation today. Also highly stimulating: Our colleague Bob Herman is out [with the opening stories](https://www.statnews.com/health-insurance-costs-out-of-pocket-out-of-reach-series/) in a new series examining how the cost of health care plans is crushing workers and businesses. Do take some time with Bob’s excellent work.

One more plug: I’ll be anchoring STAT’s pop-up newsletter from the Alzheimer’s Association International Conference here in London starting Sunday. You can sign up [here](https://www.statnews.com/signup/). Finally, to the headlines we go. …

Advertisement

**Vertex Pharmaceuticals will spend $10 billion to acquire Crinetics Pharmaceuticals and its drug for a rare endocrine disorder**, [STAT](https://www.statnews.com/2026/07/06/vertex-acquires-crinetics-pharmaceuticals/) reports. Vertex will pick up the Crinetics drug Palsonify, which was launched last year and treats a rare endocrine disorder called acromegaly, as well as other drug candidates that have blockbuster potential if approved. The company is also in the late stages of developing a therapy for congenital adrenal hyperplasia. The deal is the largest acquisition in the history of Vertex, as the Boston-based drugmaker has been on a mission to build out the next generation of products beyond its cystic fibrosis portfolio.

title: STAT+: 'Secret shopper' study probes GLP-1 telehealth sites
summary: A secret shopper investigation examined whether online telehealth platforms prescribing GLP-1 receptor agonists (anti-diabetes and obesity drugs such as Wegovy, Saxenda) conducted proper clinical evaluations before issuing prescriptions. The study was published in JAMA and authored by researchers at Yale School of Medicine, with a companion article available on the same URL structure noting that "online GLP-1 prescriptions are often fast, easy — and low on clinical oversight." Telehealth sites appear to be prescribing these high-demand medications with minimal patient assessment, raising regulatory and patient safety concerns. This matters for biotech operators and investors because it signals potential regulatory scrutiny from the FDA and DEA aimed at telehealth platforms selling weight-loss drugs, which could impact business models for digital health companies in the obesity therapeutics space.
analysis_notes: Article body was paywalled (access-gated by STAT+). Summary derived from newsletter edition description, article metadata, companion linked article titles, and related links visible on the page. The JAMA-linked companion article provides the best available detail: telehealth sites prescribing GLP-1s with fast/easy processes and low clinical oversight.

title: STAT+: America's employer health insurance is decaying
summary: Bob Herman of STAT launches a new multi-part series called "Out of Pocket, Out of Reach" examining how America's employer-based health insurance system is crumbling. The series investigates the growing affordability crisis affecting U.S. workers and businesses, with premiums rising far faster than inflation and eroding workers' earnings over time. Herman invites readers to share how job-based coverage has affected them or their businesses, suggesting the series will surface widespread personal stories of financial strain from rising insurance costs. Additional articles in the series include pieces on small businesses abandoning health insurance, an employer-insurance whistleblower profile, a takeaways roundup, and a case study of a Georgia entrepreneur struggling with ACA coverage.

The piece serves as a series launch rather than a deep-dive analysis; substantive content likely follows in individual series installments. Related Series articles visible on-page include: small business health insurance (part 1), ACA coverage cases (part 2), employer-insurance whistleblower (part 3), and a 5-takeaways roundup.

title: The crumbling employer-based health insurance system
summary: John Wilkerson's D.C. Diagnosis newsletter introduces Bob Herman's five-part series on America's decaying employer-based health insurance system, titled the "Out of Pocket, Out of Reach" investigation. The series documents rising healthcare costs driving employers and small businesses to drop coverage for workers entirely. Related articles in the same series cover Georgia entrepreneurs unable to find affordable plans, a whistleblower exposing practices at an insurance giant, online GLP-1 prescription loopholes with deficient clinical oversight, and AI-driven drug development shifts by Anthropic. The newsletter also flags topics on clinical trial diversity and 340B drug pricing trends. These stories point to structural fragmentation in the U.S. health insurance market that will likely increase patient out-of-pocket spending, reshape employer benefit strategies, and create both regulatory risk and commercial opportunity for biotech firms.

title: Another big premium hike on the horizon
summary: This STAT Morning Rounds column covers three distinct healthcare stories. First, Dexcom's Stelo continuous glucose monitor (CGM) has been cleared for over-the-counter sale to children age 2 and older — a first in the U.S. — which significantly expands accessible diabetes monitoring beyond prescription channels. Second, the article addresses unprecedented health care premium increases on the horizon, though full details are behind the STAT+ paywall. Third, it notes an FDA crackdown on online ketamine retailers. The Dexcom Stelo OTC clearance represents a meaningful expansion of at-home glucose monitoring and intensifies competition in the CGM market, with implications for consumer health markets and reimbursement models.
analysis_notes: Full article text hidden behind STAT+ paywall. Only the CGM/Dexcom Stelo body text snippet was visible; premium hike and ketamine stories only available via headline/names. Priority set to medium due to limited actionable detail. The Dexcom Stelu OTC clearance for non-prescription use by children 2+ is a verifiable regulatory milestone.

title: Pharmalittle: We're reading about a Vera kidney drug approval, a U.K. Enhertu pricing deal, and more
summary: The FDA has approved Trutakna, a new drug from Vera Therapeutics for IgA nephropathy, a kidney disease caused by immune antibody buildup. The annual list price of $425,000 positions it among the growing pipeline of therapies targeting this indication and will require meaningful insurance discounts to reach patients. Separately, U.K. officials are nearing a pricing agreement with AstraZeneca and Daiichi Sankyo that would ensure access to Enhertu, an antibody-drug conjugate extending survival by roughly six months in certain breast cancer patients; the prior CCG ruling of non-cost-effectiveness has been overtaken by updated pricing metrics tied to a U.S.-U.K. trade deal under the Trump administration. The article also notes Hong Kong-based Sino Biopharm struck two deals focused on respiratory drugs, though details are not elaborated in the free text. This roundup is filed by Jason Mast filling in for Ed Silverman from STAT's Brooklyn office.

Good morning, Jason Mast here filling in for the one and only Ed Silverman. We’re here in STAT’s Brooklyn outpost today, where everyone’s gazing across the East River to see whether Pfizer’s former headquarters will — quite literally, quite alarmingly — [crumble to the ground](https://gothamist.com/news/midtown-neighborhood-spent-a-day-in-limbo-as-structural-emergency-shut-down-blocks). It does appear things are more [stable](https://www.nytimes.com/live/2026/07/08/nyregion/nyc-midtown-building-collapse) than yesterday, which will hopefully give us time and the breathing space to focus on today’s headlines. …

**The FDA approved a new kidney disease drug from Vera Therapeutics,** [STAT](https://www.statnews.com/2026/07/07/vera-therapeutics-kidney-disease-treatment-approval/) states. The drug, called Trutakna, is designed to treat IgA nephropathy, a condition in which immune antibodies build up in the kidneys. It is one of several drugs now entering or nearing the market for the disease. Vera will charge $425,000 annually before customary insurance discounts and rebates.

Advertisement

**U.K. officials are nearing a drug-pricing deal with AstraZeneca and Daiichi Sankyo to assure access to a life-extending breast cancer drug,** [Bloomberg](https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-07-08/astrazeneca-daiichi-near-uk-price-deal-for-breast-cancer-drug) reports. The deal would allow certain women in England and Wales to receive Enhertu, an antibody-drug conjugate that has been shown to extend survival for some patients by around six months and is widely available elsewhere. The U.K. drug-pricing regulator had previously deemed the medicine not cost-effective — though that was before the U.K. changed some of its drug-pricing metrics as part of a trade deal with the Trump administration.

title: Prime Medicine claims victory in dispute over gene-editing technology with Beam Therapeutics
summary: Prime Medicine has won an arbitration ruling that it can proceed with a gene-editing therapy for the rare liver disease alpha-1 antitrypsin deficiency (AATD), after an arbitrator determined Prime's work did not violate a 2019 non-compete agreement shared with Beam Therapeutics. The two companies were spun out of the same lab and had been pitted against each other in a protracted legal dispute over overlapping gene-editing technology. The ruling clears the way for Prime to launch its AATD clinical trial this quarter, lifting Prime's stock 11% on the news. The outcome is described as a blow to Beam Therapeutics, which lost ground in the arbitration fight. This resolution removes a major overhang on Prime Medicine's development pipeline and shifts competitive dynamics in the gene-editing space.
analysis_notes: Article is behind STAT+ paywall; analysis based on the accessible headline, subhead, and first lead paragraph only. The full argumentation and specifics of the arbitration ruling are not available. The 11% stock move and clinical trial timeline for AATD appear to be material facts from the visible text.

Prime Medicine said Wednesday that it had won an arbitration dispute against Beam Therapeutics, resolving for now a [clash that had pitted two well-backed gene-editing companies](https://www.statnews.com/2025/03/19/aatd-gene-editing-prime-medicine-beam-therapeutics/) that had been spun out of the same lab against each other.

An arbitrator ruled that Prime’s work on a gene-editing drug for the rare liver disease alpha-1 antitrypsin deficiency, or AATD, did not violate a 2019 agreement designed to prevent the two companies from competing against one another.

Advertisement

The ruling paves the way for Prime to start a clinical trial for AATD this quarter, lifting the biotech’s stock 11% Wednesday morning. The outcome, however, is a blow to Beam Therapeutics.

title: Transplants extended survival for patients whose stage 4 lung cancer hadn’t spread, study says
summary: Researchers at Northwestern Medicine performed double lung transplants on patients with stage 4 lung cancer confined to the lungs who had exhausted standard treatment options, publishing results in JAMA. Historically, advanced-stage cancer has been an absolute disqualifier for transplant candidacy due to feared recurrence and poor long-term survival—but this select group demonstrated extended survival post-transplant, challenging that assumption. The study was authored by the team led by reporter Elizabeth Cooney and highlights both clinical promise and ethical dilemmas around allocating scarce donated organs to patients with a cancer diagnosis. While the sample appears small and the patient cohort highly selected (nonsmokers whose disease had not metastasize beyond the lungs), the findings suggest lung transplantation may be a viable salvage therapy for a narrowly defined population previously deemed inoperable.
analysis_notes: Full article body was behind STAT+ paywall; analysis based on headline, lead paragraphs below the paywall, and supporting sidebar context. The evidence is a single-institution observational series rather than a randomized trial—sample size, follow-up duration, and comparison arm details are unavailable without full-text access.

Could a lifesaving lung transplant strategy that contained the Covid-19 virus also save patients whose advanced lung cancer hasn’t spread?

That’s the question that inspired surgeons and oncologists at Northwestern Medicine to offer double lung transplants to patients who had run out of treatment options but whose late-stage cancer hadn’t left their lungs. People with late-stage lung cancer have not been transplant candidates before for two reasons: rates were high for cancer recurrence and low for survival.

July 8, 2026

For the second year in a row, many Affordable Care Act insurers are proposing double-digit premium increases, driven by rising medical costs as well as policy changes by Congress and the Trump administration.

In preliminary filings with state regulators, insurers are seeking a median rate increase of 14% for 2027, according to [an analysis of filings](https://www.healthsystemtracker.org/brief/how-much-and-why-aca-marketplace-premiums-are-going-up-in-2027) in 16 states and the District of Columbia by the Peterson-KFF Health System Tracker.

title: Bryan Johnson's chronic disease is notoriously difficult to diagnose
summary: Longevity entrepreneur Bryan Johnson, 48, publicly disclosed his diagnosis of autoimmune gastritis on social media, calling it a condition in which 'my stomach is eating itself.' Autoimmune gastritis is a chronic inflammatory disease where antibodies destroy acid-producing cells in the stomach, preventing iron absorption and eventually causing vitamin B12 deficiency, anemia, and neurological complications; it also carries an increased risk of stomach cancer. The article stresses that autoimmune gastritis affects an estimated 4% of people worldwide but is notoriously difficult to diagnose, with gastroenterologists often failing to recognize it—a point validated by Johnson's own experience despite his extensive health monitoring and resources. Experts quoted in the piece, including Oxford demography professor Jennifer Dowd, use Johnson's case to underscore that no amount of biohacking can fully prevent disease. Johnson is the founder of Blueprint (a longevity startup selling supplements, red-light laser therapy caps for hair loss), and co-founder of Don't Die, a self-described religion centered on health optimization; he has millions of social-media followers. His diagnosis shines attention on an under-recognized disease.
analysis_notes: Full article body was behind a paywall; summary reconstructed from headline, subheadline, excerpt paragraph, and surrounding page context. Core clinical details (autoimmune gastritis pathophysiology, 4% global prevalence, B12 deficiency risk) are present in the freely accessible teaser text, which is sufficient for an accurate analysis.

Longevity entrepreneur Bryan Johnson shared some bad news on social media recently. “I have an autoimmune disease. My stomach is eating itself,” he [wrote on X](https://x.com/bryan_johnson/status/2072069730517860385?s=46&t=TgWe29unfgxzgIzDBHCJCw). The good news? “I’m going to try and solve it.”

The disease in question is autoimmune gastritis, a chronic inflammatory condition in which antibodies destroy acid-producing cells in the stomach, which prevents people from absorbing iron. It’s not in itself a fatal disease, but it is linked to an increased risk of stomach cancer and, eventually, to deficiencies in vitamin B12, which in turn causes anemia and neurological complications.

Advertisement

Autoimmune gastritis affects an [estimated 4% of people worldwide](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/40831008/), but it may be more common than we know: Experts say the disease is notoriously difficult to diagnose, and that gastroenterologists often fail to recognize it in patients. Now it’s in the spotlight thanks to Johnson, an unexpected spokesperson for any kind of disease, given the extreme measures he takes to protect and maintain his health. A 48-year-old multimillionaire biohacker whose Blueprint startup sells supplements, red-light laser therapy [baseball caps](https://blueprint.bryanjohnson.com/products/laser-cap) to reverse hair loss, and other longevity-related wares, he’s also the founder of what he [describes as a religion](https://www.technologyreview.com/2025/05/05/1116090/bryan-johnson-new-religion-body-is-god/) called Don’t Die. In recent years, Johnson has accumulated millions of social-media followers and grabbed national headlines with his longevity efforts, at one point getting [blood plasma transfusions](https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2023-05-22/bryan-johnson-s-anti-aging-blood-transfusion-involves-dad-and-son) from his teenage son.

Johnson’s announcement is a reminder that no matter how much anyone strives to optimize their health, “you really can’t biohack your way completely out of disease or death,” said Jennifer Dowd, a professor of demography and population health at the University of Oxford.

title: In private meeting, Trump officials push to onshore generic drugmaking
summary: At a closed-door meeting at the Eisenhower Executive Office Building, Secretary of State Marco Rubio, Health Secretary Robert F. Kennedy Jr., and HHS Chief Counsel Chris Klomp urged pharmaceutical industry leaders to relocate production of critical medicines to the United States. The administration is specifically focused on supply chains for 86 drugs the Department of Health's Assistant Secretary for Preparedness and Response has classified as essential, citing fears that China or other nations could cut off U.S. access. This marks a notable intensification of White House pressure on pharma companies to onshore manufacturing amid broader concerns about geopolitical supply chain vulnerability. The article is behind a STAT+ paywall beyond this lead paragraph summary.
analysis_notes: Article is behind STAT+ paywall — only the first paragraph and subtitle are visible. The lead paragraph contains substantive details (location, attendees, 86-drug essential list, China supply chain concern) sufficient for a credible summary. Priority is high because onshoring pressure from Rubio/Kennedy signals potential regulatory or procurement leverage against pharma manufacturers.

WASHINGTON — Last week, pharmaceutical leaders filed into a meeting room in the Eisenhower Executive Office Building, next to the White House, for a meeting with Secretary of State Marco Rubio, health secretary Robert F. Kennedy Jr., and HHS Chief Counsel Chris Klomp.

The administration officials had a message for the industry: It’s time to bring production of essential medications back to the U.S. — or at least closer to home.

Advertisement

The meeting, described by an administration official and two people familiar with the event, focused on increasing U.S. control of the supply chains for the 86 medicines deemed essential by the health department’s Assistant Secretary for Preparedness and Response.
